lines.target: A Frenchman decided to adopt British citizenship. After the ceremony, which was very moving, the clerk asked him if he was proud of his new nationality. The ex-Frenchman replied:
lines.base: Un francés decidió adoptar la ciudadanía británica. Después de la ceremonia, la cual fue muy emotiva, el empleado le preguntó si estaba orgulloso de su nueva nacionalidad. El ex-francés respondió:

lines.target: Of course I am. You see, he said in a confidential tone, now I can say that we won the Battle of Waterloo.
lines.base: Por supuesto que sí. Vea usted, dijo en un tono confidencial, ahora puedo decir que nosotros ganamos la batalla de Waterloo.

lines.target: A couple of Polish émigrés to the United States had been waiting for ten years to be granted American nationality.
lines.base: Una pareja de emigrantes polacos a los Estados Unidos ha estado esperando durante diez años para que le sea concedida la nacionalidad norteamericana.

lines.target: One day, the husband came running into the kitchen, waving a letter and shouting — We're Americans! We're Americans!
lines.base: Un día, el marido vino corriendo a la cocina, agitando una carta y gritando — ¡Somos americanos! ¡Somos americanos!

lines.target: His wife turned, took off her apron and said — How wonderful! Now you can wash the dishes!
lines.base: Su mujer se dio la vuelta, se quitó el delantal y dijo — ¡Qué maravilla! ¡Ahora puedes lavar los platos tú!

lines.target: When asked if his fellow countrymen were chauvinistic, an American economist replied:
lines.base: Cuando se le preguntó que si sus compatriotas eran chauvinistas, un economista americano respondió:

lines.target: Yes and no. An American is someone who wears English suits, Italian shoes and French silk ties.
lines.base: Sí y no. Un americano es alguien que usa trajes ingleses, zapatos italianos y corbatas francesas de seda.

lines.target: He drinks Brazilian coffee, eats Swiss cheese with Austrian bread, and drives a German car.
lines.base: Bebe café brasileño, come queso suizo con pan austriaco, y conduce un coche alemán.

lines.target: Then he takes a Japanese ballpoint pen and Chinese notepaper to write to his congressman,
lines.base: Luego coge un bolígrafo japonés y papel de carta chino para escribir a su diputado,

lines.target: demanding that he do something about the foreign companies taking away our foreign markets.
lines.base: y pedirle que haga algo con relación a las compañías extranjeras que están arrebatándonos nuestros mercados extranjeros.

lines.target: Americans have a reputation for being wealthy and generous tourists — not always willingly...
lines.base: Los americanos tienen fama de ser turistas ricos y generosos (aunque no siempre voluntariamente...).

lines.target: An American tourist stopped in a tiny French village for lunch at the local inn.
lines.base: Un turista americano se detuvo en una minúscula aldea francesa para comer en una posada local.

lines.target: He ordered an omelette, which was delicious. But when the bill came, he got the shock of his life.
lines.base: Pidió una tortilla, que estaba deliciosa. Pero cuando le trajeron la cuenta, se llevó el susto de su vida.

lines.target: Tell me, he asked the owner of the restaurant, are eggs scarce in this part of the world?
lines.base: Dígame, preguntó al propietario del restaurante, ¿hay escasez de huevos en esta parte del mundo?

lines.target: No, replied the owner, but Americans are!
lines.base: De huevos, no, respondió el propietario, ¡pero de turistas americanos, sí!

lines.target: One problem that faces the British when they visit the USA is what to call "the smallest room in the house".
lines.base: Un problema que los británicos confrontan cuando visitan EU es cómo llamar «a la habitación más pequeña de la casa».

lines.target: The Americans shudder at the word "toilet"; they call it the bathroom, which for English people is where you go to wash your hands or take a bath.
lines.base: Los americanos se horrorizan ante la palabra «toilet»; ellos le llaman «el baño» (el bathroom), lo que para los ingleses es el lugar donde uno va a lavarse las manos o a tomar un baño.

lines.target: The problem is illustrated by the following true story:
lines.base: El problema queda ilustrado por esta auténtica historieta:

lines.target: In a very chic Manhattan restaurant, an Englishman visiting the USA for the first time asked the head waiter:
lines.base: En un elegante restaurante de Manhattan, un inglés que visitaba EU por primera vez le preguntó al camarero jefe:

lines.target: Excuse me, my man, where are the toilets? The waiter was outraged and replied in a haughty tone:
lines.base: Perdóneme, amigo mío, ¿dónde están los aseos? El camarero se escandalizó y respondió en un tono arrogante:

lines.target: Go up the stairs, turn right and go to the back of the room. On your left you will see a door marked "Gentlemen".
lines.base: Suba las escaleras, gire a la derecha y camine hasta el fondo del salón. A su izquierda verá una puerta con un rótulo que dice «Caballeros».

lines.target: But pay no attention. You can go in despite everything.
lines.base: Pero, descuide usted. Puede entrar a pesar de todo.

lines.target: Not all Americans are proud of their discoverer Christopher Columbus, although there is a national holiday named after him.
lines.base: No todos los americanos están orgullosos de su descubridor Cristóbal Colón, aunque exista un día de fiesta nacional que se celebra en su nombre.

lines.target: When he set off, he did not know where he was going; when he arrived, he did not know where he was;
lines.base: Cuando este partió, no sabía a dónde se dirigía; cuando llegó, no sabía dónde estaba;

lines.target: and when he returned, he could not tell anybody where he had been! Some heritage!
lines.base: ¡y cuando regresó, no pudo decirle a nadie dónde había estado! ¡Qué herencia!

gap_candidates.target: A Frenchman decided to adopt British citizenship. After the ceremony, which was very moving, the clerk asked him if he was proud of his new nationality. The ex-Frenchman replied:
Of course I am. You see, he said in a confidential tone, now I can say that we won the Battle of Waterloo.
A couple of Polish émigrés to the United States had been waiting for ten years to be granted American nationality.
His wife turned, took off her apron and said — How wonderful! Now you can wash the dishes!
When asked if his fellow countrymen were chauvinistic, an American economist replied:
Yes and no. An American is someone who wears English suits, Italian shoes and French silk ties.
He drinks Brazilian coffee, eats Swiss cheese with Austrian bread, and drives a German car.
Then he takes a Japanese ballpoint pen and Chinese notepaper to write to his congressman,
demanding that he do something about the foreign companies taking away our foreign markets.
Americans have a reputation for being wealthy and generous tourists — not always willingly...
An American tourist stopped in a tiny French village for lunch at the local inn.
He ordered an omelette, which was delicious. But when the bill came, he got the shock of his life.
Tell me, he asked the owner of the restaurant, are eggs scarce in this part of the world?
No, replied the owner, but Americans are!
The Americans shudder at the word "toilet"; they call it the bathroom, which for English people is where you go to wash your hands or take a bath.
Excuse me, my man, where are the toilets? The waiter was outraged and replied in a haughty tone:
Go up the stairs, turn right and go to the back of the room. On your left you will see a door marked "Gentlemen".
Not all Americans are proud of their discoverer Christopher Columbus, although there is a national holiday named after him.
When he set off, he did not know where he was going; when he arrived, he did not know where he was;
and when he returned, he could not tell anybody where he had been! Some heritage!
